data.title: Mercer Bancorp, Inc. SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
Mercer Bancorp, Inc., a Maryland corporation and the bank holding company for Mercer Savings Bank, has released its Form 10-Q report for the third quarter. The report highlights significant financial growth and operational performance, reflecting the company's robust business model and strategic initiatives in the banking sector.

Financial Highlights

Mercer Bancorp, Inc. reported notable financial performance metrics for the third quarter:

  • Total Interest Income: $2.33 million, reflecting an increase from the previous year due to higher loan interest income.
  • Total Interest Expense: $0.74 million, increased from the previous year primarily due to higher deposit and Federal Home Loan Bank advances expenses.
  • Net Interest Income: $1.59 million, showing growth compared to the previous year, driven by increased interest income.
  • Net Income: $0.28 million, an improvement over the previous year's net income, indicating better overall financial performance.
  • Earnings per share - basic and diluted: $0.29, up from $0.18 in the previous year, reflecting improved profitability.

Business Highlights

The company's operational performance and strategic initiatives were also detailed in the report:

  • Nature of Operations: Mercer Bancorp, Inc. serves as the bank holding company for Mercer Savings Bank, which operates primarily in western Ohio. The bank provides a variety of deposit and lending services to individual customers, including checking, savings, and term certificate accounts, as well as residential and commercial mortgage loans, agricultural loans, commercial loans, home equity lines of credit, installment loans, and indirect automobile loans.
  • Geographical Performance: The bank operates through four office locations in Celina, Ft. Recovery, and Greenville, Ohio. The performance in these regions is influenced by local economic conditions, including employment rates and real estate values.
  • Loan Portfolio: The bank's loan portfolio includes a diverse range of real estate loans, such as residential, multi-family, agricultural, commercial, and construction loans. The portfolio also includes commercial and industrial loans, as well as consumer loans like vehicle and unsecured loans.
  • Risk Management: The bank employs a standard loan grading system to monitor credit risk, categorizing loans into pass, special mention, substandard, doubtful, and loss categories. This system helps in identifying potential weaknesses and managing the credit quality of the loan portfolio.
  • Allowance for Credit Losses: The bank has adopted the current expected credit loss (CECL) model to estimate the allowance for credit losses, which is a valuation allowance deducted from the loans’ amortized cost basis to present the net amount expected to be collected.
  • Regulatory Compliance: As of December 31, 2024, the bank is categorized as well-capitalized under the regulatory framework for prompt corrective action, meeting all capital adequacy requirements.
  • Future Outlook: Management believes that the bank will continue to meet capital adequacy requirements and maintain its well-capitalized status. The bank's operations are expected to be influenced by local economic conditions, regulatory changes, and competitive pressures in the financial services industry.

SEC Filing: Mercer Bancorp, Inc. [ MSBB ] - 10-Q - Feb. 14, 2025

data.title: FTC Release: FTC Chairman Ferguson Announces New Policy Regarding American Bar Association
data.description:
Today, Federal Trade Commission Chairman Andrew N. Ferguson announced a new policy that prohibits FTC political appointees from holding leadership roles in the American Bar Association (ABA), participating in ABA events, or renewing their ABA memberships. Additionally, the FTC will no longer use its resources to support any employee's ABA membership or participation in ABA activities.

View Press Release: https://www.ftc.gov/news-events/news/press-releases/2025/02/ftc-chairman-ferguson-announces-new-policy-regarding-american-bar-association

data.title: Sector Update: Consumer Stocks Retreat in Late Afternoon Trading
data.description:
Consumer stocks were lower late Friday afternoon, with the Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLP) shedding 0.9% and the Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLY) easing 0.1%.

In corporate news, Uber launched a "seven-figure" advertising campaign to generate support for the efforts to convince state regulators to lower mandated insurance thresholds for rideshare drivers, contending those costs are increasing prices paid by its customers, Bloomberg reported. Uber shares fell 0.9%.

Airbnb shares jumped past 14% after it outlined a full-year core earnings margin outlook that analysts said was higher than expected. Late Thursday, the company logged a bigger-than-projected Q4 profit as strong demand propelled revenue above Wall Street's estimates.

TreeHouse Foods posted lower-than-expected Q4 results and a downbeat 2025 revenue outlook amid slowing growth trends for the food category. Its shares were still up 2.2%.

Lottery.com shares jumped 16% after the firm said it has signed a memorandum of understanding with PlusEVO to buy an advanced lottery management system and gaming platform to support its international expansion.

data.title: Sector Update: Energy Stocks Rise Late Afternoon
data.description:
Energy stocks rose late Friday afternoon with the NYSE Energy Sector Index up 0.6% and the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLE) adding 0.7%.

The Philadelphia Oil Service Sector index dropped 1%, and the Dow Jones US Utilities index eased 0.3%.

West Texas Intermediate crude oil declined 0.9% to $70.63 a barrel, while global benchmark Brent shed 0.5% to $74.64 a barrel. Henry Hub natural gas futures rose 2.6% to $3.72 per 1 million BTU.

In corporate news, Fortis shares rose 1.5% after the company reported Q4 adjusted earnings that topped estimates by analysts.

Diamondback Energy is in talks to buy Double Eagle in a deal potentially valuing the energy producer at over $5 billion, The Wall Street Journal reported. Diamondback shares were little changed.

Enbridge Chief Executive Officer Greg Ebel said on Bloomberg TV that the scrapped Northern Gateway oil pipeline proposal would be difficult to revive without major legislative changes in Canada. Enbridge shares fell 3.5%.

Epsilon Energy shares rose 4.3%, a day after the company's board approved a new one-year program to repurchase up to 2.2 million shares for an aggregate price of not more than $13 million.

data.title: Sector Update: Financial Stocks Advance Late Afternoon
data.description:
Financial stocks were rising in late Friday afternoon trading, with the NYSE Financial Index adding 0.3% and the Financial Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLF) up 0.2%.

The Philadelphia Housing Index was rising 0.4%, and the Real Estate Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLRE) was easing 0.3%.

Bitcoin (BTC/USD) was increasing 2% to $98,016, and the yield for 10-year US Treasuries fell 4.5 basis points to 4.48%.

In economic news, US retail sales fell by 0.9% in January, versus the 0.2% decrease expected in a survey compiled by Bloomberg and the previous month's 0.7% gain.

President Donald Trump ordered officials to develop custom tariffs for imports from all US trading partners and report their recommendations for imposing reciprocal tariffs.

In corporate news, BlackRock has approved a carried interest allocation for Chief Executive Laurence Fink, linking part of his compensation to the performance of some private market investment funds, a regulatory filing with the US Securities and Exchange Commission showed Friday. BlackRock shares were down 0.8%.

Blackstone is mulling a sale of Olympus Energy, its natural gas exploration company, that could be valued at up to $2 billion, Bloomberg reported. Blackstone shares rose 2.5%.

Brookfield Asset Management has closed its Brookfield Growth venture capital-focused unit, Bloomberg. Brookfield Asset shares added 3.6%.

HSBC is reportedly set to unveil $1.5 billion in annual cost savings as part of Chief Executive Georges Elhedery's restructuring efforts, the Financial Times reported. HSBC shares were 0.2% lower.

data.title: Moderna Norovirus Vaccine Trial Placed on Hold by FDA After Adverse Event
data.description:
Moderna said Friday its trial for a trivalent vaccine against norovirus is currently on hold by the US Food and Drug Administration after a single adverse event report of a close of Guillain-Barre syndrome.

Investigation of the adverse event is currently underway, according to the company.

Moderna said it expects the clinical hold will not impact the efficacy readout timeline for the northern hemisphere trial as enrollment has already been completed.

Shares were up nearly 4% in recent trading.

data.title: Trump says he wouldn't mind if Nippon Steel took minority stake in U.S. Steel
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump said he wouldn't mind if Japan's Nippon Steel took a minority stake in U.S. Steel , days after he announced Nippon's bid for the struggling U.S. steelmaker would take the form of an investment instead of a purchase.

Speaking to reporters at the White House on Friday, Trump said Nippon is now considering an investment, including debt, in U.S. Steel.

"Minority stake I wouldn't mind greatly," Trump said in response to a question on whether he'd support a minority stake by Nippon in the U.S. company.

"What they're doing right now is they're going to be investors, and they're going to be investing in, I think, debt and various other things," he added.

Nippon's $14.9 billion pursuit of U.S. Steel has stretched on for more than a year. The proposal became highly politicized ahead of November's U.S. presidential election, with both former President Joe Biden and Trump pledging to kill it.

Last week, Trump said he would be involved in a deal "to mediate and arbitrate."

Earlier this week, a Japanese government spokesperson said Nippon is considering proposing a bold change in plan from its previous approach of seeking to buy U.S. Steel.

data.title: Wayne County Jury Awards $1.7 Million to Injured Deputy and His Wife After Three-Day Trial
data.description:
a Wayne County jury found in favor of the plaintiff, Brent Butler, in his personal injury case against a driver who struck him while he was directing traffic. In addition to this just award, they found the defendant 100% at fault for the plaintiff’s injuries.

Nashville, Feb. 14, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — a Wayne County jury found in favor of the plaintiff, Brent Butler, in his personal injury case against a driver who struck him while he was directing traffic. In addition to this just award, they found the defendant 100% at fault for the plaintiff’s injuries.

Case background

Brent Butler is a career law enforcement officer who was working with the Wayne County Sheriff’s Office at the time of the incident. Butler was directing traffic at the scene of a single-vehicle crash when a distracted driver struck him. The impact sent Butler airborne, and he landed on his head, causing a traumatic brain injury. He still requires ongoing medical care because of this severe injury.

Legal proceedings

Rocky McElhaney Law Firm represented Butler and his wife, Gina, and brought the case to trial. The defense attempted to place blame on another driver who stopped or slowed down ahead of the scene. The trial took place over three days in Wayne County, with the jury deliberating for 2.5 hours before returning the verdict at about 10 p.m. on Friday, February 7, 2025. The jury found the defendant 100% liable and awarded the plaintiffs $1.7 million for Butler’s injuries.

The pretrial offer from the defense was for just $400,000. The jury’s decision reflects the serious and lasting impact of Butler’s injuries.

Impact on Brent Butler and law enforcement officers

While Butler was able to return to a job in law enforcement, he continues to struggle with the ongoing symptoms of a TBI. He remains under neurological monitoring for his condition.

We are proud to have reached this successful verdict, which highlights the importance of protecting law enforcement officers on duty. Rocky McElhaney Law Firm is an award-winning personal injury law firm dedicated to fighting on behalf of injury victims and challenging big insurance companies that work to avoid paying fair compensation. We are located in downtown Nashville, East Nashville, Hendersonville, Clarksville, and Knoxville. Contact Rocky McElhaney Law Firm for information or further inquiries regarding the case.

data.title: Chicago wheat hits four-month high as cold threatens Russia, US crops
data.description:
Key points:
  • Wheat futures rise on short covering, winterkill worries
  • Corn, soy futures gain on weather risks
  • Investors relieved Trump hasn't imposed reciprocal tariffs yet

Chicago Board of Trade wheat futures soared on Friday to their highest price since October, and analysts said support from blistering cold weather in the Black Sea and U.S. Plains regions prompted short covering.

Corn and soy futures also ticked up as traders monitored parched weather in Argentina, a major producer of both crops.

Grain and soy futures were broadly supported by an easing dollar and investor relief that U.S. President Donald Trump did not immediately impose reciprocal tariffs globally, analysts said.

Trump's decision to order his economics team to prepare plans for reciprocal tariffs was seen as leaving scope for negotiations, though traders remained wary of a possible trade war and retaliation against U.S. agricultural exports.

"The fact that he's giving plenty of negotiating time is friendly for markets," said Randy Place, analyst at Hightower Report.

Most-active CBOT wheat settled up 22-1/4 cents at $6 per bushel, reaching a high of $6.02-3/4 per bushel.

The most-active corn contract settled up 2-3/4 cents at $4.96-1/4 per bushel, and CBOT soybeans settled up 6 cents at $10.36 per bushel.

Cold temperatures in Russian and U.S. hard red winter wheat areas could cause freeze damage to dormant crops that will miss out on a layer of insulating snow cover, traders said. Russia is the world's biggest wheat exporter.

In France, the soft wheat crop has already worsened sharply since December, data from farm office FranceAgriMer showed.

Supply concerns have prompted commodity funds, which hold a large net short position in wheat, to cover their short positions, traders said.

Corn and wheat markets were also buoyed by brisk weekly U.S. exports, though wheat import tenders this week from Algeria and Saudi Arabia were expected to show continued competition for business from the Black Sea region.

data.title: Global Energy Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1537 ET - Oil futures are unable to hold onto early gains fueled by Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent's comments on Fox News about the U.S. reducing Iran's oil exports to 100,000 barrels a day. "It's highly unlikely we actually achieve that feat," Alex Hodes of StoneX says in a note. It implies a drop of 1.4 million barrels a day, which would push up prices, but "this would be difficult to implement as Iran has become more adept at sanctions evasion techniques," he adds. WTI falls 0.8%, to $70.74, for a 0.4% weekly loss. Brent settles down 0.4%, at $74.74, up 0.1% on the week. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1459 ET [Dow Jones]--The U.S. natural-gas rally stretches to a fifth straight day as the market braces for another bout of Arctic weather that's expected to lift heating demand next week, along with the risk of production freeze-offs. "This is a technically driven market purely based on weather," says long-time natural-gas trader John Woods, who expects the rally could still have some upside left to it. "I see a little bit more of a push. I think we're going to get an overreaction," he says. The Nymex front month settles up 2.7% at $3.725/mmBtu for a 13% gain on the week. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1334 ET - The number of rigs drilling for oil in the U.S. edged up by one this week to 481, which was 16 fewer than a year ago, oil services company Baker Hughes reports, while natural gas rigs increased by one to 101, down 20 from a year before. In the Dallas Fed's 4Q energy survey published last month, two-thirds of exploration and production executives who anticipate increasing investment this year beyond their previous estimate said they expect to invest more in drilling and completion of wells. A majority of executives also expect permitting times for drilling wells on federal lands to decrease over the next four years. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1156 ET - Republic Services expects to benefit from its new recycling center, located in Indiana, in the second half of 2025, CEO Jon Vander Ark says on a call with analysts. The waste management company has completed construction at the Indiana Polymer Center and equipment commission is underway, Vander Ark says. Overall for next year, the company is looking at incremental revenue of around $70 million, and incremental earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization of $35 million across its portfolio of sustainability investments. The company also expects to spend another $75 million on Polymer Centers this year. Regarding its renewable natural gas projects, the company says it expects to have seven new projects in 2025. Shares rise 3.1%, to $231.34. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1143 ET - The U.S. aims to squeeze Iranian oil exports as part of its "maximum pressure" strategy on Tehran, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent says in a Friday interview with Fox Business. "We are committed to bringing the Iranians to going back to the 100,000 barrels-a-day of oil exports," he says. Iran is currently exporting 1.5 million-1.6 million barrels per day, with export revenue used to fund terrorist activities, according to Bessent. "The Chinese, perhaps the Indians, are buying the sanctioned Iranian oil and that is unacceptable." Bessent says cutting Iran's oil exports and intensifying economic pressure will push the regime into severe economic distress. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

1007 ET - Oil futures are higher in early trading and looking to post their first weekly gain in four weeks. The U.S. delay in imposing reciprocal trade tariffs globally and a slightly less bearish outlook from the IEA this week offset some of the downward pressure from a bigger-than-expected build in U.S. crude stocks and President Trump's talk with Russia's Vladimir Putin on starting negotiations to end the Russia-Ukraine war. Today traders focused on Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent's comments about reducing Iran's exports to 100,000 barrels a day. WTI is up 0.4% at $71.56 a barrel and Brent is 0.6% higher at $75.47 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0930 ET - U.S. natural gas futures are on track for a second straight weekly gain with the return of freezing weather this week and next supporting expectations of further inventory declines, while flows to LNG export facilities are at record highs. "Very cold mid-to-late February weather could send the storage deficit towards 300 Bcf in early March," Eli Rubin of EBW Analytics says in a note. Natural gas inventories were 67 Bcf below their five-year average as of Feb. 7, according to the EIA's latest report. The Nymex front month is up 2.3% at $3.711/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0921 ET - The wave of reciprocal tariffs proposed by President Trump is raising the ire of trading partners globally. On the green energy side, Brazilian ethanol producers are protesting the potential measures, arguing that they 'make no sense' when comparing U.S. and Brazilian ethanol. "The measure aims to put ethanol produced in Brazil and the United States on the same level, even though they have different environmental attributes and decarbonization potential, and therefore it makes no sense to talk about reciprocity," says UNICA, a Brazilian trade group representing the sugarcane and renewable energy industries. U.S. ethanol uses corn as its main feedstock, while Brazilian ethanol uses sugarcane. Ethanol is a major consumer of corn for U.S. producers. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0858 ET - A resolution to the Russia-Ukraine war by U.S. President Trump could be a boon for Europe, likely resulting in a joint EU issuance fund for reconstruction and development, Nomura analysts say. While real money clients cannot invest in bonds allocated to defence spending, they can invest in bonds allocated to reconstruction, the analysts say in a note. "Therefore, we could see a significant increase in joint EU issuance in 2025, which we believe would be well-received by investors," the analysts say. Nomura would also expect the bulk of winning tenders for rebuilding Ukraine to be European companies. "This would be a boon for European construction and manufacturing sectors, both of which have been languishing since the 2022 gas crisis." (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0817 ET - The euro could rise as much as 5% against the dollar if a Ukraine ceasefire is agreed followed by the resumption of gas supplies, J.P. Morgan analysts say in a note. However, whether this is realized will depend on the details, they say. Negotiations will require tough decisions on territories and de-militarization zones, Ukraine's neutrality, security guarantees and enforcement mechanisms. The resumption of lower-cost gas supplies, if at all, will likely be a smaller proportion compared to before Russia invaded Ukraine in 2022. The euro is also at risk of U.S. trade tariffs, while the U.S. economy remains strong. "A ceasefire wouldn't mean European's structural problems will disappear." The euro earlier hit a two-and-a-half-week high of $1.0491, according to FactSet. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0816 ET - TC Energy's 4Q performance at first blush looks to be neutral, with results coming in line with consensus expectations and guidance for 2025 unchanged, Jefferies' Anthony Linton says. Adjusted Ebitda was C$2.6 billion, as a lower-than-expected contribution from TC's natural gas pipelines was offset by a higher contribution from the power business thanks to a strong performance at the Bruce nuclear facility. Alongside the results, the company announced a 3.3% rise in its quarterly dividend, implying an annual yield of about 5.1% and in line with 2025 forecasts, Linton says. Jefferies has a C$71 target on the shares, which last closed at C$67.20. (robb.stewart@wsj.com)

0758 ET - European airlines are likely to benefit from a Ukraine-Russia ceasefire, if the deal leads to the reopening of Russian airspace to overflights for European carriers, JP Morgan analyst Harry Gowers says. A ceasefire could also cause downward pressure on jet fuel prices, improved air traffic control, and tighter European capacity, he writes in a research note. However, it would initially put pressure on air freight rates, harming logistics companies, he says. The outcomes for Wizz Air and Lufthansa would range from neutral to very positive, Gowers adds. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

data.title: Crude Futures Little Changed After Volatile Week — Market Talk
data.description: Oil futures are unable to hold onto early gains fueled by Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent's comments on Fox News about the U.S. reducing Iran's oil exports to 100,000 barrels a day. "It's highly unlikely we actually achieve that feat," Alex Hodes of StoneX says in a note. It implies a drop of 1.4 million barrels a day, which would push up prices, but "this would be difficult to implement as Iran has become more adept at sanctions evasion techniques," he adds. WTI falls 0.8%, to $70.74, for a 0.4% weekly loss. Brent settles down 0.4%, at $74.74, up 0.1% on the week. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: Sector Update: Health Care
data.description:
Health care stocks declined late Friday afternoon, with the NYSE Health Care Index and the Health Care Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLV) each shedding 0.9%.

The iShares Biotechnology ETF (IBB) eased 0.2%.

In corporate news, enGene shares fell 8% after UBS downgraded the stock to neutral from buy and cut its price target to $7 from $34.

data.title: CAC 40 Posts Strong Weekly Gains
data.description:
The CAC 40 edged up 0.2% to close at 8,178 on Friday, extending gains for the fifth consecutive session and securing a 2.9% weekly rise, buoyed by strong Q4 earnings.

Hermès added 0.8% after reporting a 17.6% rise in Q4 sales to €3.96 billion, exceeding expectations.

Its peers followed suit, with LVMH and Kering gaining 0.8% and 1.7%, respectively.

Conversely, Sanofi, AXA, and ArcelorMittal led the laggards, losing between 1.2% and 1.7%.

Meanwhile, traders continued to evaluate the potential impact of the trade war as President Trump announced country-specific tariffs set to begin in April, with investors awaiting further details and hoping for a negotiated resolution.

data.title: Golden Matrix Group Insider Purchases 300,000 Shares
data.description:
Reporter NameMilovanovic Aleksandar
Relationship10% Owner
TypePurchase
Amount$555,000
SEC FilingForm 4

Aleksandar Milovanovic, a 10% owner of Golden Matrix Group, has directly purchased a total of 300,000 shares of common stock on February 12 and 13, 2025, at a price of $1.85 per share, amounting to a total purchase of $555,000. Following these transactions, Milovanovic directly owns 78,314,616 shares of Golden Matrix Group.

SEC Filing: Golden Matrix Group, Inc. [ GMGI ] - Form 4 - Feb. 14, 2025

data.title: Dollar Declines on Falling US Yields
data.description:
The dollar index on Friday fell by -0.52%, posting a new 2-month low and adding to Thursday’s decline of -0.58%. The dollar was undercut by Friday’s weak US retail sales report and a -5 bp decline in the 10-year T-note yield, which added to Thursday’s decline of -9 bp. Also, there were continued fears about a global trade war after President Trump on Friday said that new tariffs on autos are coming around April 2.

Friday’s US Jan retail sales report of -0.9% was weaker than expectations of -0.2%. Excluding autos, Jan retail sales fell by -0.4%, weaker than expectations of +0.3%. Jan “control group” retail sales, which excludes volatile sectors, fell by -0.8%, weaker than expectations of +0.3%. However, the retail sales report was undercut to some extent by the wildfires in California and severe weather in various areas of the country.

In other US economic news, US Jan industrial production rose +0.5% m/m, stronger than expectations of +0.3%. However, Jan manufacturing production fell by -0.1%m/m, weaker than expectations of +0.1% m/m.

US Jan import prices rose by +0.3% m/m and +1.9% y/y, which was close to expectations of +0.4% m/m and +1.9% y/y. Excluding petroleum, US Jan import prices rose by +0.1% m/m, weaker than expectations of +0.2%.

The markets are discounting the chances at 2% for a -25 bp rate cut at the next FOMC meeting on March 18-19.

EUR/USD fell -0.34%, adding to Thursday’s decline of -1.05%. The yen continued to benefit from hawkish comments Thursday from Japanese Prime Minister Ishiba, who said the Japanese economy is on its way to achieving sustainable inflation backed by wage growth. 

April gold (GCJ25) on Friday closed down -44.70 (-1.52%), while March silver (SIH25) closed up +0.129 (+0.39%). Precious metals saw support from the weaker dollar and the continued decline in the 10-year T-note yield. However, gold prices saw long liquidation pressure after the sharp rally seen in the past two months. Gold failed to see much support from the Thursday night drone attack on the Chernobyl nuclear plant in Ukraine.

On the date of publication, Rich Asplund did not have (either directly or indirectly) positions in any of the securities mentioned in this article. All information and data in this article is solely for informational purposes. For more information please view the Barchart Disclosure Policyhere.

data.title: Wayne County Jury Awards $1.7 Million to Injured Deputy and His Wife After Three-Day Trial
data.description:
Nashville, Feb. 14, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — a Wayne County jury found in favor of the plaintiff, Brent Butler, in his personal injury case against a driver who struck him while he was directing traffic. In addition to this just award, they found the defendant 100% at fault for the plaintiff’s injuries.

Case background

Brent Butler is a career law enforcement officer who was working with the Wayne County Sheriff’s Office at the time of the incident. Butler was directing traffic at the scene of a single-vehicle crash when a distracted driver struck him. The impact sent Butler airborne, and he landed on his head, causing a traumatic brain injury. He still requires ongoing medical care because of this severe injury.

Legal proceedings

Rocky McElhaney Law Firm represented Butler and his wife, Gina, and brought the case to trial. The defense attempted to place blame on another driver who stopped or slowed down ahead of the scene. The trial took place over three days in Wayne County, with the jury deliberating for 2.5 hours before returning the verdict at about 10 p.m. on Friday, February 7, 2025. The jury found the defendant 100% liable and awarded the plaintiffs $1.7 million for Butler’s injuries.

The pretrial offer from the defense was for just $400,000. The jury’s decision reflects the serious and lasting impact of Butler’s injuries.

Impact on Brent Butler and law enforcement officers

While Butler was able to return to a job in law enforcement, he continues to struggle with the ongoing symptoms of a TBI. He remains under neurological monitoring for his condition.

We are proud to have reached this successful verdict, which highlights the importance of protecting law enforcement officers on duty. Rocky McElhaney Law Firm is an award-winning personal injury law firm dedicated to fighting on behalf of injury victims and challenging big insurance companies that work to avoid paying fair compensation. We are located in downtown Nashville, East Nashville, Hendersonville, Clarksville, and Knoxville. Contact Rocky McElhaney Law Firm for information or further inquiries regarding the case.

Name: Alexis McCoy

Email: alexis@rockylawfirm.com

Job Title: Chief Marketing Officer

data.title: Primaris REIT Brief: Keeps Sector Perform, Target Edges Up To C$17.25 From $17 On "Strong" Q4 Results and Outlook, But Says "Macro Volatility Looms in the Background"
Dollar on track for weekly loss against euro as tariffs delayed
data.description:
Key points:
  • Tariffs, Russia-Ukraine optimism weigh on greenback
  • US retail sales fall more than expected in January

The dollar was on track for a weekly loss against the euro on Friday as a delay in the introduction of trade tariffs planned by U.S. President Donald Trump raised hopes that they may not be as bad as feared, while optimism about a peace deal between Russia and Ukraine helped the single currency rally.

The dollar index also fell to a nine-week low after data showed that retail sales fell more than expected in January, leading traders to raise bets that the Federal Reserve may cut rates two times this year.

“Markets are still hoping that tariff headwinds are not going to be as significant as perhaps previously feared, then probably the bigger element this week is enthusiasm about potential Russia-Ukraine ceasefire and to what extent that might be positive for European growth in particular,” said Vassili Serebriakov, an FX strategist at UBS in New York.

“The retail sales are probably a tertiary factor, but it's kept the dollar on the back foot,” Serebriakov said.

The euro rose 0.32% to $1.0497 and got as high as $1.0514, the highest since January 27. It is on pace for a weekly gain of 1.7%.

The Japanese yen strengthened 0.37% against the greenback to 152.22 per dollar.

The dollar index was last down 0.35% at 106.72 and on track for a weekly loss of 1.3%. It reached 106.56, the lowest since December 12.

Trump on Thursday tasked his economics team with devising plans for reciprocal tariffs on every country that taxes U.S. imports. Howard Lutnick, Trump's pick for commerce secretary, said the administration would address each affected country one by one and said studies on the issue would be completed by April 1.

The broad announcement appeared designed at least in part to trigger talks with other countries, with a White House official saying Trump would gladly lower tariffs if other countries lowered theirs.

U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent also said on Friday that the Trump administration is looking beyond tariffs and non-tariff barriers to examine currency manipulation as it studies the issue.

Analysts say that tariffs could add to inflation, keeping the dollar higher as the Fed holds rates higher for longer.

Trump also said on Friday he plans to impose tariffs on imported cars around April 2.

The euro and other European currencies have been supported this week by optimism that Russia and Ukraine will reach a peace deal.

Trump discussed the war in Ukraine on Wednesday in phone calls with Russian President Vladimir Putin and Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy.

Friday’s retail sales data, meanwhile, comes after Thursday’s producer price report for January pointed towards lower than previously thought core Personal Consumption Expenditures Price Index later this month, which is the Fed’s preferred inflation measure.

Futures traders are now pricing in 41 basis points of cuts for the year, fully reversing a move towards reduced rate cut expectations after consumer price data came in hotter than expected on Wednesday.

In cryptocurrencies, bitcoin gained 1.32% to $97,781.23.
